sentences.text: They were cut as the war started, right in the beginning.
There were no communications.
No telephone, no [INAUDIBLE], no telegraph, nothing.
I mean, I-- I practically didn't know what is happening to my family.
I didn't know what happens to my-- to a few blocks away from us.

We tried to stay home as much as possible and to get, little by little, from time to time, out, and to get some supply, some food, because it was very dangerous to get out in the street, because in many cases-- many occasions where the people went out to the street, they-- they would never come back.
We had some relatives on the-- we had a high part of Kaunas, which was the-- the Green Mountains.
That was the name.
And some relatives-- the family of my aunt was married to my-- to my uncle-- lived there.
And one of her brothers had a-- a farm.
So, at one point, one evening we decided to move out from the place where we're staying and to go to their-- to their house.
And fortunately-- when we came there, we heard terrible things.
They were assaulted.
They were taken away-- everything-- all the belongings.
And they wanted to kill and to take away my aunt's brothers.
But fortunately, they had some watches, and they were able to bribe the-- those people who came in.
They were completely drunk, you know.

That's-- you know, Lithuanians, they-- the-- the non-Jews there were at a-- at a-- at a level of-- very low level of-- of intelligence, in a certain way, I would say that, because they were-- many of those-- first of all, drinking was a-- a national-- a national--
Right, pastime.
Every time-- like we have parties here, and there the party started with drinking, being drunk, and then go out and fight.
This was their-- their pastime, basically.
So now they had a opportunity.
They didn't have any supervision.
They were-- they were-- they felt that suddenly they became the-- the-- the power of the country.
And-- and there was nobody to stop them.
But they were-- they felt they had their arms.
They had-- they had-- there were the pistols.
They had the guns.
And they were able to terrorize anybody.
So it was actually very difficult to commute.
So we went to-- Incidentally, my parents, before they-- a few months earlier, they were not sure whether they are going to be taken out to Russia.

So they brought certain of their goods, like my mother had a very beautiful coat you know, from chinchilla and some other things, they brought that and they left that in in the capital, because they felt it's always-- if they're going to be taken out, they should preserve it.
So we took-- took certain things and we took it to my aunt's parents, where they lived on the-- they used to live on this Green Mountain.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we've-- we came over there.
And when we heard what happened to those people, we came-- we went back to our apartment.
And we felt that the safer place was to stay there and and just keep a low visibility.
We were also fortunate because the-- the place where we lived, the apartment, the manager, a Lithuanian, was a very nice Lithuanian man who was in his-- I mean, he was older person.
And he was really in very good-- we-- we had a very good relationship.
He used to come to collect the rent and so forth and so on.
And we-- And he, truthfully-- I mean, our-- our life was-- our destiny was in his hands.
And he was very nice.
And he would warn us and tell us when we can go out and get something to-- buy something to-- for-- to eat, or when we should stay away.
So--
They came in, I would say, about a week after-- a week or 10 days after the war started.

They came in, very-- first of all, they took many, many prisoners, POWs, a tremendous amount, because their tactic was they-- they surrounded-- amazingly, they came from the north, rather than from the-- from the-- from the south or the west, southwest.
They came up to Kaunas because they they had their system, the-- to encircle, and then to squeeze.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And this is basically what they have done.
They came in with-- with their motorcycles and with their armor, and nicely polished shoes, you know, boots.
And-- and you can-- right away, we-- we were able to feel the-- the-- the German presence.
I mean, they-- they were very regulated and very orderly.
And, of course, everything had to be to the-- I mean, properly done.
And it was-- although they were very bad in in many aspects, but at least they had a certain authority, even more with the Lithuanians.
They would let them function, but they-- they-- they had impressed upon them and the Lithuanians felt that there is a a power above them, that they just cannot do anything what they want.
Of course, related to Jews, I mean, the-- the Germans would turn their head, you know?
OK.
But-- but still, it was a little bit more-- a little better organized.
At least we were able to know exactly the different rules that they start instituting, one after the other one.
Befehl, that is in German, they were right after one befehl after the other one.

And it was like I mentioned to you, turn over all the valuables, that Jews cannot walk on the sidewalk, they have to walk-- the Jews can only walk during certain hours of the day they can go out, that Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cannot come into-- to stores.

They have only a certain hour to come into the stores, that they're limited to a certain amount of money, they're limited to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- they took basically away all all the rights.
The Jews suddenly became like nothing.
I mean, like dust.
You know.
No, we had-- we had-- they re-instituted the Lithuanian currency, because, as you know, when the Russians came, they had this currency changed to rubles and chervontsy.
That was the money.
And now they have re-instituted the Lithuanian, but they also brought in the German-- later on, the German-- German marks.
It was--
It was-- and in order to survive, what we had to do is we had to take some of our belongings and to exchange that for bread or for anything.

And and those people who had certain valuables-- beyond the fact that they have-- they have turned in, but certain people have risked their lives and they still kept certain valuables, which later on helped them to-- to survive in a certain way.
Afterwards, I mean, they-- the next step was that many-- in this-- in this interim time, there were thousands were killed and murdered, actually.
There was a case in a gas station where the Lithuanians have been drinking for a long time.
And they assembled a group of about 25 Jews.
Within them was my teacher of mine.
And they put in hoses, in their mouth, and they just-- I mean, they-- they poured gasoline and-- and water through their system, and-- and they just killed them.
In way-- It was atrocious.
The prisons were also filled up with so-called Communists and and Jews.
So it was, truthfully-- to get over this short period of time and-- and to still be alive was actually a miracle.
Now, the next befehl [BACKROUND NOISE] was that the Jews have to assemble.
They have to take their belongings, and-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] and they have to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they have to get assigned a place in the ghetto.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was also very difficult to find horses and the-- and wagons in order to transport.
So it took us some time in order to be able to get somebody that would do that.
And we had to pay him a much higher price, you know, for that.
And finally, we had-- we were able and we felt that we are going to be more secure in the ghetto, because at least it's fenced around.
And, of course, we didn't envision what is going to come afterward.
But at least we felt that the the Lithuanians would not have a-- a easy way to come in and to murder the Jews.
You know.
So finally we came to-- and we were able to get in the ghetto.
We were assigned a small two rooms for a family, plus the fact we have some other people who lived in the same-- in the same house.
It was one common kitchen for everybody.
And we were right away told that we have [BACKROUND NOISE] to start forming a a committee inside the ghetto.
And we had like a elder, which was Dr.
Elkes, who was the-- the head of the committee.
And there were some [BACKROUND NOISE] people assigned.
Now, when we came into the ghetto, we were around 30-- 30,000-some Jews.
And we occupied a-- a large section, which was inhabitated by-- it was in a Jewish section, but there were many Lithuanian who lived there.
This was the famous Slabodka, which, as you know, there was a Slabodka yeshiva was in that part of Kaunas.
It was like a-- a small suburb.
And they pushed in in a-- in a place where you can hold maybe 5,000 people, they pushed in 30- 30,000-some people in that in that place.
The ghetto was walled in, fenced around with barbed wire, and with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with policemen and guards surrounded, watching the ghetto, watching the people should not come in.
We-- we didn't have the right to come in and out, except with a-- with a-- with a guard.
This way was instituted the ghetto.
And, in order to-- to make sure that everybody's behaving, what they did is they-- they start putting out some new-- new commands, new befehl.
Like, you had to give-- [BACKROUND NOISE] turn in everything from letter.
You had to turn in all kind of secondary things, which they consider they're also valuable, you know?
And what they did is they took one Jew and they hanged him.
His name was Mek, M-E-K.
And they pointed out that this Jew didn't turn in some of his valuables.
And that's the reason why they hanged him publicly.
And-- and he was hanging for seven days, in-- in a-- in a very noticeable spot, so the people are going to understand that they mean business.
[BACKROUND NOISE] There was also a very interesting incident, which affected me personally.
And this was right when-- when they closed the ghetto.
They called for 500 [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] intelligent people.
And I don't mean 'intelligent' of saying being intelligent, but people who had-- educated people, I would say.
They need them-- they claim they need them for-- needed them for a very-- very special assignment.
And, of course, people start to run, because they felt that this must be-- and also, they promised that they are going to be-- their assignment is going to be very special.
They are going to have extra food, and they are going to have extra shelter.
So everybody start running to this place.
Myself, I was going with my-- with my aunt's brother, who was going with me.
And for some reason, he-- he got delayed for a little while.
And he was doing something in-- in-- inside the apartment.
And we came-- when we came, [BACKROUND NOISE] there were some people who were faster, apparently, than us.
And they told us, that's it.
I mean, when they said, 500, they had, like, 503 people.
That's it.
And many people have pushed themselves.
They-- they wanted to be there.
And can you believe this?
Well, the first 500 people that they took out and they shot them the next day, all of them.
They machine-gunned all those 500 people.
And the reason is, what they did is-- and this was--
You see, the system that they had, this was applied to each ghetto, which we found out later.
To each-- each ghetto, each camp, that they had, was they used the same-- the same method, same system.

What they wanted to do is actually clean the ghetto from people who were a little bit better educated, you know, so they were not going to have this-- the uprisings or something that is going to affect the-- the-- the-- their grip on the-- on the ghetto.
Oh, we knew that, yeah.
We knew that, because there-- there-- Kaunas was surrounded by four forts, a fort.
And one of the forts was the Ninth Fort.
And there was still some communication between Lithuanians, who used to come to the-- to the-- to the fence.
And people used to trade with them, give them, like, a-- a-- a coat or a shirt in exchange for butter or for milk, or for bread, especially.
And many of those people were also without shame.
They used to take a stone-- butter wrap the stone, and say, that's a kilo butter.
And then, when you found-- when you-- and you had to do that very fast, because apparently the-- the guard should not see that you are trading, you know?
And then you find out that you were fooled, you know?
That's-- on top of the heavy stones was little bit of butter, you know.
So it was-- they-- they took all kind of measures in order to fool the people.

So with the way we knew is, we didn't have communication but we had, like, mouth-to-mouth communication, where the Lithuanians would come-- some of them who had friends who were-- there were exceptions to the rule.
There were some Lithuanians who were very friendly with some of the people, and they really felt for them, you know.
So they-- they were the ones that told us.
And the-- and the people who lived in-- in-- surrounding the fort, they heard shooting.
And-- and then they had to bury them, you know.
They used to dig out mass graves with a bulldozer, and they used to bury them there.
Were there work details?
Pardon?
Oh, yes, of course.

They-- right away they instituted-- you know, the biggest concern of theirs and what they needed right away to do is to put back the airport in-- in the-- in good condition so they can use that for the Luftwaffe, you know.
And the-- the first brigades, working brigades, that were instituted were the-- to the airport.
Everybody had to work-- to go to work to the airport.
If somebody was sick or he didn't feel good, he had to find somebody else.
Like I, for instance, I at that time-- this was 1941, and I was 17 at that time, so I worked for myself.
My uncle was a sick man.
And I-- I used to work a second shift, which was, like, 10 hours, another 10 hours to work for my uncle.
And it was a-- we had to walk under guard.
It was, like, eight, nine miles to the airport, back and forth, about 18 miles.
And ration was very meager.
I had, like, a piece-- 200 grams of bread.
And then they-- they gave us little bit of soup, which was just water, you know.
It didn't contain any fat or any-- any-- any anything.
And the weather was very harsh.
The winter was very cold.
And they wouldn't let you warm up, you
Know?
We had some fires, from-- in certain spots.
They would let you, for a minute, you know, to come and to-- they said, you warm up by working.
You don't warm up by-- by holding [BACKROUND NOISE] your hands.
And so that was basically-- that was the first-- the first working condition were to the-- to fix-- to fix the airport.
I mean, to-- How long did you remain in the ghetto?
I remained in the ghetto three years, you know, from 1941 to 1944, a little bit less than three years.
Conditions get worse and worse and worse.
Because they start using different types of aktionen.
One of them-- in October 28, 1941, they had a mass selection.
And this happened in-- all over, in every single ghetto, whether in Poland or in Czechoslovakia or in-- or in Belgium.
I mean, this was their tactic.
They took out-- the-- the selection was that in the morning they had announced that everybody has to go out to the special-- to a special place, you know, where open space.
And they would check-- they claimed that, if somebody is going to remain inside the houses, I mean, they are going to be shot immediately.
So everybody should go out, and-- early, [BACKROUND NOISE] like 5 o'clock in the morning, and wait for further instructions.
So everybody came out, and we were-- it was the full ghetto, which was about 30,000-some people.
It was a huge, huge amount.
And they started--
Then the Gestapo came with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rauca was at that time the head of the Gestapo.
And he came with a group of Nazis, with dogs.
And they used to-- the selection-- go to the left, or go to the right.
And we found out right away-- you didn't have to be a genius to find out that those who were going to the left are going to be taken out from the ghetto somewhere.
Nobody knew where or what.
But-- because based on the composition-- they were all elderly people, children, too young people to work.
You know, they-- those who were able to work, they put them to the right.
And they took me to the right.
I had my grandmother, who was that time in the-- close to 60.
And when the German turned away, I pulled her back over to my side.
And fortunately, you know, there was such a mass of people, that they-- they hardly-- But then they separated, took out, about 10,000 people, 12,000 people.
And they took them out, and they took them also to the Ninth Fort.
And they-- they-- they were killed in the-- the next-- the next day.
So the ghetto became an--
That was the first selection.
That was the first-- the first large selection.

Then they had-- then they-- we had a small ghetto where we had a hospital, where we had to cross a bridge over this street, [BACKROUND NOISE] because they made the certain streets, certain roads-- they-- there was very-- they had movement, and they were just not able to include them in the ghetto.

What they did is, they built a bridge, protected bridge, whereby they could communicate-- a small ghetto, was called a 'small section'-- when they have to go cross over the-- like a crossover bridge, you know?
And there-- there was the hospital.
And one day they just came and they stopped the crossover.
They surrounded the hospital.
They locked in the doctors and-- and everybody else there.
They put gasoline and they burned everybody alive there, in that-- in that hospital.
The sick and the doctors and nurses, everybody else, they went up in smoke.
And then they took this part of the ghetto-- they cancelled this overpass, and they just gave that back to the Lithuanians.
And, little by little, they start-- the ghetto start shrinking.

We had also some factories inside the ghetto, where people used to work and fix uniforms and had a-- a shoe repair and uniforms and all kind of-- all kind of repairs for the German-- for the German army people.
Yeah, that's right.
Yeah.
That's-- later on, I started work in this factory.
And this was very good to me, because I was able to-- to steal some marmalade and sugar and some other thing, bring the-- bring into the ghetto.
And, this way, we-- I was able-- we were able to survive, because the meager ration that they gave us, I mean, this was just absolutely impossible to-- to-- to live on.
No.
No.
We knew that something terrible happened to all the small cities.

But we were not-- we were aware sometimes after-- after the turning between-- after the 1941, we we knew that-- because some soldiers, and some Jewish soldiers who were able to escape and who came to certain-- Lithuanian army-- came-- and especially one that was stationed in-- in-- in our city said that everybody in the city was taken out and killed.
They were taken out to a secluded place, about three kilometers.
And, first of all, they separated the men.
And then they had the men in a seclusion, and then the women, children, and elderly people separately.
So first of all, in-- in August, they claim sometimes in the middle of August, they took out the men, and they took them to [NON-ENGLISH].
That was a little forest, a young forest.
And they dug there-- they made them dig their graves, and they would-- they shot them.
And then a couple days later, like, seven, eight days later, they took the children, the women, and the-- and the elderly people.
And they-- they were also shot, and they had the same fate the men.
We-- every time they came with the new-- new ideas-- like there was, later, the children actually, were by-- there were still some children who were hiding.
And they-- they came in when the men and women were out to work.
They came in and they surrounded the ghetto, came in with-- with autobuses, which were the-- the windows were tinted-- and-- that you cannot look inside.
And they came out, and they took-- they gave the order to the police.
We had a Jewish police in the ghetto.
Some of them-- most of them were good, but a few of them, very bad, that they worked-- they tried to please their boss or the Germans, and they would do terrible acts.
So they came in, and they-- and they came out with loudspeakers, saying that everybody has to stay home.
They cannot move out.
First of all, they took the police.
And I had-- my wife-- my aunt's brother was a police officer, very nice guy.
And they made him sit down on the ground.
And then they asked him to get up.
And-- and my uncle, my aunt's brother was-- I mean, he-- I actually saw the-- what's happening was, it gave him some kind of convulsion.
He was not able.
So he was shot to death right away.
The German pulled out a pistol and shot him.
Then they took all the police, and they put them in one of the buses.
And then they took all the children and the elderly people.
And that was the Kinder Aktion, which was called the [GERMAN].
They took out all the little one.
When their mothers came back from work, later on, this was a terrible, terrible, terrible outcry, you know?
And it was just something that you couldn't [INAUDIBLE].
Some mothers were-- who were not feeling well, and they wanted to go with their children, they wouldn't let them.
Some were shot at the spot, you know, when they tried to take away the children, the mothers wouldn't let them do that.
I mean, they would-- they would say, we want to go where-- the fate of the child should be our fate.
So that-- this-- this was a very terrible experience.
So the ghetto was-- became smaller and smaller, start shrinking.

And the-- also the front, the things, the happenings on the-- on the front with Russia, when they were-- when Stalin said that my best general is General Winter, start affecting the German-- the German composure and the German-- the German strength, in the sense that suddenly it came Stalingrad, and it came, you know, all the defeats that they had on the-- on the eastern front.
Things have-- the mood of the Germans have start changing.
And they were start being pushed back to-- little by little, their victory became actually start more sounding as a defeat.
So they tried-- but the Nazis and Hitler placed more importance to the killing and to the annihilation of the Jews than to-- than-- than what happened, really, in the front.
So things have start changing dramatically.
And this was-- we were now toward the end of 1943.
Very good.
[BACKROUNDN NOISE] Terrific.
